# Orientation: From Notebook to Enterprise AI System

<span class="part-kicker">Part 0</span>

This part sets the operating context for the book. The central move is from notebook work to enterprise AI delivery: a model or prompt becomes useful only when it sits inside a system with users, data contracts, identity, evaluation, monitoring, governance, and support.

## Why Start Here

Industry AI work fails when teams treat production delivery as an afterthought. Before learning APIs, Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG), Model Context Protocol (MCP), or governance templates, you need a map of how the pieces connect.

<section class="flow-strip">
  <article class="flow-step"><span class="flow-step__label">Reader</span><p>Clarify who the book is for and what background it assumes.</p></article>
  <article class="flow-step"><span class="flow-step__label">Map</span><p>See how ML, LLMs, backend systems, security, and delivery fit together.</p></article>
  <article class="flow-step"><span class="flow-step__label">Case</span><p>Anchor the book in a secure enterprise document Q&A assistant.</p></article>
  <article class="flow-step"><span class="flow-step__label">Path</span><p>Choose the reading sequence that matches your role and urgency.</p></article>
</section>

## What You Should Be Able To Explain

- Why a notebook result is not a production system.
- Why enterprise AI systems need identity, auditability, evaluation, and support.
- How the document Q&A assistant connects to ML foundations, RAG, APIs, security, agents, and delivery.
- Which reading path fits your current role.

## Read Next

Start with **Who This Book Is For**, then read the delivery map and case study before moving into ML foundations.
